text.content: MINI PRD: P30 — How to Customize User Experience
Status: Plan
Batch: 3 | Priority: P3 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 800-1500
What This Article Does Now

Covers customizing the FluidFlow user experience — environments, unit sets, visible results, flowsheet preferences, and saved layouts. Likely thin content focused on menu paths. Missing opening statement, "So What?", Quick Reference, glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Be the reference for making FluidFlow feel like YOUR tool — custom unit sets, saved environments, preferred result displays, and flowsheet appearance settings. Engineers who customize their setup are stickier users.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement**: "FluidFlow adapts to your preferred working style. Customize unit systems, result displays, flowsheet appearance, and calculation defaults — then save your setup as a reusable environment so every new project starts the way you want it."

**Add Quick Reference table**: Customization area | Menu path | Shortcut — covering: Unit sets (Options → Environment), Visible Results (F8), Calc Options (F2), Result Units (F9), Flowsheet settings (View menu).
**Add "So What?" closing**: "A personalized setup eliminates repetitive configuration and ensures every project starts with your preferred standards — saving time on every model you build."
**Add section on saving and sharing environments**: Explain how to export/import environments so team members use consistent settings.
**Add section on custom warning thresholds**: Link to P10 (Analyze Results) for warning configuration detail.
**Add glossary links** (see below).
**Add meta description**: "Customize FluidFlow's unit systems, result displays, and calculation defaults. Save your preferred setup as a reusable environment for consistent project standards."
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Environment](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#environment) — opening section
[Data Palette](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#data-palette) — visible results section
[Flowsheet](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#flowsheet) — appearance settings
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P04 UI Overview, P10 How to Analyze Results, P25 How to Export Reports
BLOG: 1 relevant post
CTA: Build Your First Network — https://knowledge.fluidflowinfo.com/en/articles/13238470-build-your-first-network-in-fluidflow
Selling Message
0 — Customization reference. No selling message fits naturally.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Reporting: configurable velocity/pressure limits, Excel/PDF/BOM
UOM: SI, US Basic, custom environments
Alerts: 118 configurable warnings and hints
MINI PRD: P31 — Composite Pump Curves
Status: Plan
Batch: 4 | Priority: P31 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2000-3000
What This Article Does Now
Explains series and parallel pump configurations in FluidFlow. Likely includes basic definitions and setup steps. May lack engineering context on when/why to use each configuration.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Teach engineers how to visualize and analyze composite pump curves for multi-pump systems. Must explain WHEN series vs parallel configurations are appropriate (head vs flow requirements), HOW FluidFlow generates composite curves, and WHY this matters for system reliability and efficiency. Include practical examples: duty/standby configurations, booster systems, high-flow applications.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering problem (sizing multi-pump systems), what the reader will learn, name FluidFlow's composite curve tool.

**Add engineering context section**: Explain series pumps (additive head at constant flow) vs parallel pumps (additive flow at constant head) with 2-3 sentence explanations of when each is appropriate.
**Add practical examples**: Include at least 2 scenarios (e.g., booster pumps for high-rise buildings = series; duty/standby for reliability = parallel).
**Add visualization guidance**: Explain how to interpret FluidFlow's composite curve plots — where operating point falls, how to identify inefficient configurations.
**Add Best Practices section**: Common mistakes (mismatched pump curves in parallel, incorrect sequencing), validation checks (individual vs composite NPSH).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting composite curves to system reliability and capital cost optimization.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in operating point section
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — in curve interaction section
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in validation section
[Head](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#head) — in series pump section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page)
SIDEWAYS: P36 Check Pump Curve Fit, P38 Modeling a Centrifugal Pump, P34 Equipment Performance Tool
DOWN (glossary): Duty Point, System Curve, NPSH, Head
BLOG: Pump selection articles if available
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

2 — "System-level thinking" — weave into opening: FluidFlow automatically generates composite curves so engineers can evaluate multi-pump configurations at the system level, not just component-by-component. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Pump modeling capabilities (auto-sizing, curve database)
Equipment Performance Tool
System Curve interaction
Multiple pump configurations (series/parallel/duty-standby)
Visualization/charting tools
MINI PRD: P32 — Hydraulic & Energy Grade Lines
Status: Plan
Batch: 4 | Priority: P32 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2000-3000
What This Article Does Now
Introduces HGL and EGL concepts. Likely covers basic definitions and plotting in FluidFlow. May lack depth on how to USE these visualizations for troubleshooting and design validation.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Teach engineers how to interpret HGL/EGL plots as visual diagnostic tools for system energy distribution. Must explain WHAT each line represents physically (HGL = pressure + elevation, EGL = HGL + velocity head), HOW to identify problem areas (negative pressure, excessive losses), and WHY this visualization is essential for avoiding cavitation, surge, and inefficient designs.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering problem (visualizing energy distribution in piping systems), what the reader will learn, name FluidFlow's HGL/EGL plotting capability.
**Add conceptual foundation section**: Explain HGL vs EGL with physical meaning (not just formulas). Use 1-2 analogies (e.g., "HGL shows available pressure energy at each point").
**Add interpretation guidance**: How to spot problems from HGL/EGL plots — negative HGL (cavitation risk), steep EGL drops (excessive losses), EGL below HGL (impossible, indicates calc error).
**Add practical examples**: Include 2-3 scenarios (e.g., pump suction side analysis, gravity flow validation, identifying bottleneck locations).
**Add Best Practices section**: When to use HGL/EGL analysis (complex systems, elevation changes, pump placement), common misinterpretations.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting HGL/EGL visualization to preventing costly field failures and optimizing energy efficiency.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Hydraulic Grade Line](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#hydraulic-grade-line) — in conceptual section
[Energy Grade Line](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#energy-grade-line) — in conceptual section
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in cavitation discussion
[Head](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#head) — in energy components section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page)
SIDEWAYS: P33 EGL/HGL Advanced System Analysis, P42 Can I plot Energy & Hydraulic Grade Lines?, P38 Modeling a Centrifugal Pump
DOWN (glossary): Hydraulic Grade Line, Energy Grade Line, NPSH, Head
BLOG: System analysis articles if available
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

2 — "System-level thinking" — weave into closing: FluidFlow's automatic HGL/EGL plotting enables engineers to evaluate system behavior holistically, catching issues that component-by-component analysis would miss. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Liquid, Gas, Two-Phase modules (all support HGL/EGL)
Pressure calculation methods
Elevation handling
Visualization/charting tools
Pump NPSH calculations
MINI PRD: P33 — EGL/HGL Advanced System Analysis
Status: Plan
Batch: 4 | Priority: P33 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2000-3000
What This Article Does Now
Advanced treatment of HGL/EGL analysis. Likely builds on P32 with more complex scenarios. May lack structured methodology for applying these tools to real design challenges.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Teach advanced techniques for using HGL/EGL plots in complex system design and troubleshooting. Must cover multi-pump systems, elevation-dominated systems, compressible flow considerations, and iterative design optimization using HGL/EGL feedback. Target audience: experienced engineers working on complex industrial systems.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the advanced challenges (complex networks, multiple pumps, elevation changes), what the reader will master, name FluidFlow's advanced HGL/EGL capabilities.
**Add prerequisite note**: Link to P32 for foundational concepts — this article assumes HGL/EGL basics are understood.

**Add advanced scenarios section**: Cover 3-4 complex cases (e.g., multi-pump systems with composite curves, systems with significant elevation changes, gas systems with compressibility effects, two-phase flow HGL/EGL interpretation).

**Add iterative design methodology**: Show how to USE HGL/EGL plots to guide design iterations (e.g., identifying optimal pump placement, sizing pipes to maintain adequate HGL, balancing branch flows).
**Add troubleshooting section**: How to diagnose specific problems from HGL/EGL patterns (e.g., flat HGL = choked flow, oscillating EGL = calculation instability).
**Add Best Practices section**: When advanced HGL/EGL analysis is warranted, computational considerations (mesh/segment size), validation against field data.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting advanced HGL/EGL analysis to preventing million-dollar design failures in complex industrial systems.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Hydraulic Grade Line](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#hydraulic-grade-line) — in opening
[Energy Grade Line](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#energy-grade-line) — in opening
[Choked Flow](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#choked-flow) — in gas systems section
[Two-Phase Flow](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#two-phase-flow) — in two-phase section
Internal Links
UP: P32 Understanding Hydraulic & Energy Grade Lines (foundation)
SIDEWAYS: P31 Composite Pump Curves, P34 Equipment Performance Tool, P41 Two-Phase Choked Flow
DOWN (glossary): Hydraulic Grade Line, Energy Grade Line, Choked Flow, Two-Phase Flow
BLOG: Complex system case studies if available
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

4 — "Validated against real-world systems" — weave into troubleshooting section: FluidFlow's HGL/EGL calculations are validated against published data and field measurements, giving engineers confidence in complex design decisions. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
All modules (Liquid, Gas, Two-Phase, Slurry)
Multi-pump configurations
Elevation handling
Compressible flow (Gas module EOS solver)
Two-phase flow regime mapping
Visualization/charting tools
MINI PRD: P34 — Equipment Performance Tool
Status: Plan
Batch: 4 | Priority: P34 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now
Describes the Equipment Performance Tool feature. Likely covers basic usage steps. May lack clear explanation of WHEN to use this tool vs other FluidFlow analysis methods.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Provide step-by-step guidance for using FluidFlow's Equipment Performance Tool to evaluate how pumps, compressors, and fans perform across operating ranges. Must explain WHAT this tool does (plots equipment curves against system curves), WHEN to use it (equipment selection, off-design analysis), and HOW to interpret results (operating point stability, efficiency zones).
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering task (evaluating equipment performance across operating ranges), what the reader will accomplish, name the Equipment Performance Tool.
**Add "When to Use This Tool" section**: Distinguish from standard analysis — use this for equipment selection, off-design evaluation, troubleshooting existing systems.
**Add step-by-step procedure**: Clear numbered steps with screenshots/descriptions (select equipment, define operating range, generate performance plot, interpret results).
**Add interpretation guidance**: How to read the output (operating point, efficiency curves, stable vs unstable regions, turndown capability).
**Add practical examples**: Include 2 scenarios (e.g., pump selection for variable flow system, fan performance at altitude).
**Add Common Issues section**: Typical user errors (wrong equipment curve data, system curve not updated, misinterpreting multi-speed curves).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting Equipment Performance Tool to confident equipment selection and avoiding costly mis-sizing.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in operating point section
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — in curve plotting section
[Efficiency](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#efficiency) — in interpretation section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page)
SIDEWAYS: P31 Composite Pump Curves, P36 Check Pump Curve Fit, P38 Modeling a Centrifugal Pump
DOWN (glossary): Duty Point, System Curve, Efficiency
BLOG: Equipment selection articles if available
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

3 — "Fastest path from concept to confident design" — weave into step-by-step section: The Equipment Performance Tool provides immediate visual feedback, accelerating equipment selection without external spreadsheet analysis. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Pump modeling (curve database, auto-sizing)
Compressor/fan modeling
System Curve generation
Interactive charting/visualization tools
Operating point calculation
MINI PRD: P35 — Back Calc Input Tool
Status: Plan
Batch: 4 | Priority: P35 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now
Describes the Back Calc Input Tool (reverse calculation feature). Likely explains basic operation. May lack clear use cases showing WHEN reverse calculation is necessary.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Provide step-by-step guidance for using FluidFlow's Back Calc Input Tool to solve for unknown system parameters when target outputs are specified. Must explain WHAT reverse calculation means (solving for pipe size, pump head, etc. given desired flow/pressure), WHEN it's needed (retrofit analysis, design constraints), and HOW to set up and interpret results.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering problem (determining required equipment/pipe sizes to meet performance targets), what the reader will accomplish, name the Back Calc Input Tool.

**Add "When to Use This Tool" section**: Clear use cases (e.g., sizing pipes for target pressure drop, determining required pump head for specified flow, retrofit analysis where constraints are fixed).
**Add step-by-step procedure**: Numbered steps (select target variable, specify desired output, configure constraints, run back-calculation, review results).
**Add interpretation guidance**: How to verify results are realistic (e.g., check velocities, validate NPSH, ensure equipment is within standard sizes).
**Add practical examples**: Include 2-3 scenarios (e.g., "What pipe size gives 50 psi at outlet?", "What pump head achieves 500 GPM through existing piping?").
**Add Common Issues section**: Typical problems (over-constrained systems, non-physical solutions, multiple valid solutions).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting Back Calc to efficient retrofit design and meeting project constraints without trial-and-error.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Head](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#head) — in pump sizing section
[Pressure Drop](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#pressure-drop) — in pipe sizing section
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in validation section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page, Scripting Module section)
SIDEWAYS: P34 Equipment Performance Tool, P38 Modeling a Centrifugal Pump, P47 Scripting Handbook
DOWN (glossary): Head, Pressure Drop, NPSH
BLOG: Design optimization articles if available
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

3 — "Fastest path from concept to confident design" — weave into opening: Back Calc Input Tool eliminates iterative trial-and-error, solving directly for required parameters to meet design targets. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Scripting Module (Back Calc Input Tool is part of this module)
Reverse calculation capability
All modules (feature available across Liquid, Gas, Two-Phase, Slurry)
Auto-sizing capabilities (related functionality)
MINI PRD: P36 — Check Centrifugal Pump Curve Fit
Status: Plan
Batch: 4 | Priority: P36 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now
Explains how to verify pump curve data accuracy in FluidFlow. Likely covers curve fitting methods. May lack guidance on acceptable fit tolerances and what to do when fit is poor.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Provide step-by-step guidance for validating that manufacturer pump curve data is accurately represented in FluidFlow models. Must explain WHY curve fit matters (garbage in = garbage out), HOW to check fit quality (visual inspection, residuals, R² values), and WHAT to do when fit is inadequate (adjust curve points, request better vendor data, use polynomial fit).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering problem (ensuring accurate pump representation from vendor data), what the reader will accomplish, name FluidFlow's pump curve fitting tools.
**Add "Why Curve Fit Matters" section**: Explain consequences of poor fit (wrong operating point, incorrect NPSH, inefficient design). 2-3 sentences.
**Add step-by-step procedure**: Clear numbered steps (enter pump curve data, plot curve, review fit statistics, adjust if needed, validate against vendor chart).
**Add quality criteria section**: Define acceptable fit (e.g., R² > 0.99, visual inspection for monotonic behavior, residuals < 5%).

**Add troubleshooting section**: Common curve fit problems (non-monotonic data, sparse data points, shut-off head errors) and solutions (add intermediate points, use polynomial vs point-to-point, contact vendor).
**Add practical examples**: Include 1-2 scenarios (e.g., vendor provides only 3 points — how to validate, pump curve shows wiggle — how to fix).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting accurate curve fitting to reliable pump selection and avoiding field performance surprises.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in operating point section
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in validation section
[Efficiency](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#efficiency) — in curve data section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page)
SIDEWAYS: P38 Modeling a Centrifugal Pump, P39 Model Manufacturer's Pump, P34 Equipment Performance Tool
DOWN (glossary): Duty Point, NPSH, Efficiency
BLOG: Pump modeling articles if available
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

4 — "Validated against real-world systems" — weave into quality criteria section: FluidFlow's curve fitting tools ensure vendor data is accurately translated into calculations, matching real equipment performance. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Pump modeling capabilities (curve entry, polynomial fit, database)
Liquid and Slurry modules (both use centrifugal pumps)
Visualization/charting tools
Pump curve database (vendor data)
MINI PRD: P37 — Modeling Fan and Blower Performance Curves
Status: Plan
Batch: 4 | Priority: P37 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2000-3000
What This Article Does Now

Covers fan and blower modeling using manufacturer data. Likely includes data entry steps. May lack guidance on handling different curve formats (static vs total pressure, standard vs actual conditions).
What It Should Do After Refactor

Teach engineers how to accurately model fan and blower performance in FluidFlow using manufacturer data sheets. Must explain WHAT data is needed (pressure-flow curves, efficiency, speed), HOW to handle different vendor formats (static/total pressure, standard/actual density), and WHY proper modeling matters (accurate system sizing, energy consumption predictions). Include gas property effects (density, temperature) on fan performance.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering challenge (accurately representing fan/blower performance with varying density and system conditions), what the reader will learn, name FluidFlow's fan modeling capabilities.

**Add data preparation section**: Explain vendor data variations (static pressure vs total pressure curves, standard air conditions vs actual, single vs multiple speed curves). Provide guidance on converting between formats if needed.
**Add step-by-step procedure**: Clear numbered steps (extract data from vendor chart, enter into FluidFlow, configure density/temperature conditions, verify curve fit, run system analysis).
**Add gas property considerations section**: Explain how density changes affect fan performance (fan laws), when to use standard vs actual conditions, temperature/altitude corrections.
**Add practical examples**: Include 2-3 scenarios (e.g., HVAC system at altitude, process gas blower with varying temperature, induced draft fan sizing).

**Add Best Practices section**: Common mistakes (using wrong pressure basis, ignoring density effects, mismatched speed curves), validation checks (efficiency curves reasonable, operating point in stable region).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting accurate fan modeling to energy-efficient designs and avoiding fan surge/stall issues.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Static Pressure](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#static-pressure) — in pressure basis section
[Total Pressure](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#total-pressure) — in pressure basis section
[Density](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#density) — in gas properties section
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in operating point section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page, Gas module)
SIDEWAYS: P34 Equipment Performance Tool, P36 Check Pump Curve Fit, P38 Modeling a Centrifugal Pump (parallel structure)
DOWN (glossary): Static Pressure, Total Pressure, Density, Duty Point
BLOG: HVAC or gas system articles if available
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

6 — "All flow regimes" — weave into gas properties section: FluidFlow's Gas module accurately handles fan performance across subsonic to near-sonic conditions using full equation-of-state calculations. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Gas Module (compressor/blower/fan modeling)
Equation of State calculations (density effects)
Auto-sizing for fans/blowers
Vendor database (fan curves)
Visualization/charting tools
MINI PRD: P38 — Modeling a Centrifugal Pump
Status: Plan
Batch: 4 | Priority: P38 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now

General guide to centrifugal pump modeling in FluidFlow. Likely covers basic setup. May lack structured approach to different data scenarios (full curve vs single point, vendor data vs generic selection).
What It Should Do After Refactor

Provide comprehensive step-by-step guidance for modeling centrifugal pumps in FluidFlow from various starting points. Must cover multiple scenarios: using vendor curve data, auto-sizing from scratch, selecting from FluidFlow database, modeling from single duty point. Include validation checks (NPSH, efficiency, curve behavior) and common mistakes.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering task (representing centrifugal pump performance in system model), what the reader will accomplish, name FluidFlow's pump modeling capabilities.
**Add "Before You Start" section**: What information you need (flow requirement, head requirement, fluid properties, vendor curve if available), when to auto-size vs use specific pump.

**Add multiple pathway guidance**: Clear decision tree or sections for different scenarios (Pathway A: Have vendor curve data → see P39; Pathway B: Auto-size from requirements → follow auto-size steps; Pathway C: Select from database → follow database steps).
**Add step-by-step procedure for each pathway**: Clear numbered steps with decision points.
**Add validation section**: Mandatory checks after pump entry (NPSH available > NPSH required, efficiency reasonable, operating point on stable part of curve, shutoff head > system max).
**Add Best Practices section**: Common mistakes (ignoring viscosity effects, wrong fluid properties, unrealistic efficiency assumptions), when to consult pump manufacturer.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper pump modeling to avoiding cavitation, inefficient operation, and costly equipment failures.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in requirements section
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in validation section
[Head](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#head) — in requirements section
[Efficiency](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#efficiency) — in validation section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page)
SIDEWAYS: P39 Model Manufacturer's Pump, P36 Check Pump Curve Fit, P31 Composite Pump Curves, P34 Equipment Performance Tool
DOWN (glossary): Duty Point, NPSH, Head, Efficiency
BLOG: Pump selection articles if available
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

5 — "1,283 fluids database" — weave into fluid properties section: FluidFlow automatically calculates pump performance with accurate fluid properties from its 1,283-fluid database, eliminating manual corrections. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Liquid Module (primary pump modeling module)
Pump auto-sizing capabilities
Pump curve database (multiple vendors)
NPSH calculations
Slurry Module (centrifugal pump solids derating methods)
MINI PRD: P39 — How to Model a Manufacturer's Pump
Status: Plan
Batch: 4 | Priority: P39 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now
Explains how to enter specific manufacturer pump data into FluidFlow. Likely covers data entry from vendor curves. May lack guidance on handling incomplete data or unusual vendor formats.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Provide detailed step-by-step guidance for entering specific manufacturer pump curve data into FluidFlow models. Must cover reading vendor datasheets, extracting curve points, entering data into FluidFlow, validating accuracy, and troubleshooting common vendor data problems (incomplete curves, inconsistent units, multiple speed data).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering task (using specific vendor pump instead of generic selection), what the reader will accomplish, when to use manufacturer data vs auto-size.

**Add "Reading Vendor Datasheets" section**: Brief guide to extracting data from typical pump curves (H-Q curve, efficiency curve, NPSH curve, power curve). Include tips for digitizing charts if only PDF available.

**Add step-by-step procedure**: Clear numbered steps (locate vendor curves, extract key points, enter into FluidFlow pump element, configure speed/impeller diameter, verify curve fit, run validation checks).
**Add data quality section**: How many points to extract (minimum 5-7 recommended), handling multiple speed curves, dealing with incomplete NPSH or efficiency data.

**Add troubleshooting section**: Common vendor data problems (inconsistent units, curves don't match fluid properties, multiple impeller sizes on same chart, efficiency curve doesn't align with H-Q curve) and solutions.
**Add validation checks**: Verify curve fit (see P36), check NPSH, compare operating point efficiency to vendor chart, validate power consumption.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting accurate vendor data entry to confident pump selection and avoiding mis-application.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in operating point section
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in validation section
[Efficiency](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#efficiency) — in curve data section
[Impeller](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#impeller) — in configuration section
Internal Links
UP: P38 Modeling a Centrifugal Pump (parent how-to)
SIDEWAYS: P36 Check Pump Curve Fit, P34 Equipment Performance Tool, P31 Composite Pump Curves
DOWN (glossary): Duty Point, NPSH, Efficiency, Impeller
BLOG: Pump selection articles if available
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

NONE — This is a focused how-to. Include only if it fits naturally: "FluidFlow includes pump curves from major manufacturers in its database, but custom entry ensures exact vendor model representation."
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Pump modeling capabilities (manual curve entry)
Pump curve database (for comparison/validation)
Curve fitting tools (see P36)
NPSH calculations
Visualization/charting tools
MINI PRD: P40 — Modeling Vacuum Pumps with Vendor Data
Status: Plan
Batch: 4 | Priority: P40 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2000-3000
What This Article Does Now

Covers vacuum pump modeling using vendor data. Likely addresses unique challenges of vacuum systems. May lack depth on absolute vs gauge pressure handling, volumetric flow corrections, and vacuum-specific performance curves.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Teach engineers how to model vacuum pumps in FluidFlow using manufacturer data, addressing unique vacuum system considerations. Must explain WHAT makes vacuum pump modeling different (absolute pressure regime, volumetric flow at suction conditions, gas load vs pumping speed), HOW to properly enter vendor data (handling ACFM vs SCFM, ultimate pressure, pumping speed curves), and WHY proper modeling matters (achieving target vacuum, sizing for gas load, avoiding pump overload).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering challenge (modeling vacuum systems with absolute pressures and volumetric flow corrections), what the reader will learn, name FluidFlow's vacuum pump modeling approach.
